questions.question: What must a true beginner understand and practice before independently creating valid meaningful HTML documents?
acceptanceEvidence: Current standards and beginner curriculum authorities map to explicit explanations, worked examples, early editing, faded practice, debugging, and changed-case assessment.

questions.question: Which current HTML, CSS, accessibility, responsive-design, and certification objectives require original teaching and practice?
acceptanceEvidence: Every versioned external objective maps to an original competency and complete introduce-through-transfer learning sequence.

questions.question: Which inline editing, preview, diagnostics, testing, recovery, and evidence features let learners build and verify web artifacts accessibly?
acceptanceEvidence: Tablet and desktop learner tasks pass with Monaco or native fallback, sandboxed preview, semantic diagnostics, changed-case checks, persistence, and keyboard or assistive-technology use.

intendedLearners: True beginners and returning learners who need a complete path from their first HTML element to independently designed accessible responsive sites.
entryCompetencies: Use keyboard or equivalent input, open a supported browser, and follow accessible file and navigation instructions.
terminalTasks: Design, build, test, and defend an original multi-page responsive website for an unfamiliar stakeholder and varied content.
Diagnose semantic, layout, accessibility, responsive, performance, and interaction defects from browser evidence and verify durable repairs.
Explain and justify HTML structure, CSS architecture, responsive choices, accessible behavior, and tradeoffs without relying on copied workshop recipes.
scopeIncludes: Computer, file, browser, URL, source, DOM, accessibility-tree, layout, and developer-tool foundations introduced only where needed for immediate building
HTML syntax, elements, content, nesting, attributes, documents, metadata, text semantics, landmarks, links, images, media, lists, tables, forms, validation, and native interaction
CSS syntax, cascade, inheritance, specificity, selectors, values, units, box model, typography, color, backgrounds, borders, effects, layout, positioning, Flexbox, Grid, and custom properties
Intrinsic and responsive design, media and container queries, responsive media and typography, preferences, motion, accessibility, debugging, testing, and maintainability
Original workshops, faded practice, debugging clinics, independent labs, reviews, quizzes, five certification projects, delayed assessment, and portfolio defense
scopeExcludes: Framework-specific component systems, production backend services, and JavaScript application architecture beyond bounded native web behavior
authenticTasks: Build an accessible public information site whose semantic structure remains useful before CSS and across assistive technology.
Build and test a data-collection experience with clear labels, instructions, validation, error recovery, and responsive layout.
Create a content-heavy reference site that withstands long text, localization, zoom, forced colors, missing resources, and varied viewports.
Diagnose a broken responsive interface using source, DOM, computed-style, layout, accessibility, network, and console evidence.
Defend an unfamiliar stakeholder site against semantic, accessibility, responsive, performance, and maintainability acceptance criteria.
knownMisconceptions: HTML is visual formatting text rather than a parsed structure that communicates meaning and relationships.
A tag name alone proves semantic correctness regardless of content model, nesting, accessible name, relationship, or context.
Responsive design means selecting a few device widths instead of building content-driven resilient systems.
Passing a source substring check proves rendered structure, behavior, accessibility, and changed cases work.
Accessibility can be added after visual completion without changing structure, content, interaction, or testing.
safetyBoundaries: Learner HTML, CSS, and JavaScript render only in an opaque sandboxed preview with bounded navigation, output, and no same-origin access.
Canonical hidden checks and solutions remain server-side; learner submissions are evidence rather than answer authority.
Projects use fictional or learner-owned content and never require unsafe publication, credentials, tracking, external contact, or unauthorized assets.

claims.statement: HTML syntax, elements, content models, document conformance, forms, media, tables, and author requirements control technical correctness.
limitations: Living Standard is authoritative technical scope but is not a novice teaching sequence or evidence that a learner can apply requirements.

triggers: WHATWG changes an element, content model, conformance rule, form behavior, or API used in course material.

claims.statement: CSS Snapshot identifies the stable CSS specification surface while linked modules control detailed syntax and behavior.
limitations: Snapshot status does not establish browser interoperability, beginner sequence, accessibility, or project usefulness for each feature.

triggers: W3C publishes a newer CSS Snapshot or changes status of a feature taught as stable.

claims.statement: Perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust outcomes require semantic, keyboard, focus, status, reflow, contrast, target, input, and motion evidence.
limitations: Conformance does not prove overall usability, inclusive content quality, learner understanding, or compatibility with every assistive technology.

triggers: W3C publishes a superseding WCAG Recommendation or applicable normative correction.

claims.statement: Beginner front-end scope includes standards, semantic HTML, CSS, responsive design, accessibility, testing, browser knowledge, collaboration, and professional practice.
limitations: MDN supplies curriculum outcomes and learning resources but not this platform's complete interactive practice, assessment, correction, or credential evidence.

triggers: MDN changes curriculum modules, learning outcomes, assessment guidance, or Learn Web Development structure.

claims.statement: Responsive Web Design v9 provides the required external topic and practice-depth benchmark across lectures, workshops, labs, reviews, quizzes, projects, and examination.
limitations: Names, counts, and source curriculum do not prove quality and cannot be copied as learner-facing prose, scenarios, code, assessments, or solutions.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes v9 objectives, blocks, challenge inventory, projects, or examination structure.

decisions.title: Use current standards as technical authority
decisions.rationale: External curricula establish coverage expectations, while WHATWG and W3C standards control technical claims and MDN helps translate essential professional outcomes.
validation: Every taught technical rule must trace to a reviewed authority and pass current-browser changed-case tests where behavior matters.

decisions.title: Match v9 coverage with original learning work
decisions.rationale: Certification breadth and thousands of small interactions set the minimum depth target, but every explanation, scenario, artifact, check, project, and solution must be original and meaningfully varied.
validation: Granular objective mapping, semantic duplication review, human lesson inspection, and beginner pilots must pass without token-count shortcuts.

decisions.title: Teach HTML through immediate bounded construction
decisions.rationale: True beginners need explicit syntax and mental models tied to immediate editing; long abstract tooling sequences must not delay creating and inspecting their first HTML structure.
validation: Opening learner flow must explain elements, tags, content, nesting, attributes, and documents while meaningful editing begins within two workshop steps.

decisions.title: Keep accessibility active in every later build
decisions.rationale: Semantic structure, keyboard use, naming, status, reflow, contrast, input, preferences, and assistive-technology evidence are continuing correctness constraints, not one isolated module.
validation: Later CSS, responsive, animation, lab, project, and exam work must retain and re-test previously introduced accessibility competencies.

decisions.title: Grade parsed and observed web behavior
decisions.rationale: Source substrings cannot establish document relationships, accessible names, rendered behavior, responsive adaptation, or survival under changed content and preferences.
validation: Checks must parse structure, inspect computed behavior, exercise changed cases, and reject malformed, misplaced, inaccessible, or deleted retained work.

openQuestions: Granular current freeCodeCamp v9 objective mapping must be reviewed for pedagogical meaning rather than inventory parity.
HTML beginner sequence needs expert and novice review before generator expansion.
CSS module order needs dependency review against current specifications and beginner misconceptions.
Five certification project briefs need originality, independence, accessibility, and rubric-validity review.
Monaco and native editor flows need observed keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, touch, recovery, and tablet testing.
Sandbox, diagnostics, canonical checks, changed-case execution, persistence, reset, and checkpoint boundaries need threat and failure review.

triggers: Referenced standards, MDN curriculum, freeCodeCamp v9 inventory, browser support, Monaco support, WCAG, or learner evidence changes.
